```python
# If the import path contains a colon, we split into path and class name.
# We must distinguish the drive-letter colon in Windows paths (e.g., "C:/...")
# from the class-separator colon in "filepath:ClassName" notation.
# Path.drive is unreliable cross-platform, so we check directly:
# a single ASCII letter before a lone colon means a Windows drive letter.
colons = import_path.count(":")
if colons > 1 or (colons == 1 and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive):
```

**修改后**：
```python
# If the import path contains a colon, we split into path and class name.

# from the class-separator colon in "filepath:ClassName" notation.

if colons > 1 or (colons and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive):
```

requirements.location:
src/griffe/finder.py — module level (new class definition) and functions
      _handle_pth_file (line 376) and _handle_editable_module (line 399)
requirements.description:
`_handle_pth_file` and `_handle_editable_module` currently return `list[Path]`.
      The tests access `.path` on each element returned by these functions (e.g.,
      `[sp.path for sp in _handle_pth_file(...)]`), but `pathlib.Path` objects have no `.path`
      attribute, causing `AttributeError: 'PosixPath' object has no attribute 'path'` in 7 tests.
      A dedicated `SearchPath` wrapper type is missing.
requirements.contract:
Define a `SearchPath` class (or named tuple / dataclass) in `src/griffe/finder.py` with at
      minimum the following interface:
        - `path: Path` — the filesystem path this search entry represents
        - `always_scan_for: str | None` — optional package name that must always be scanned for in
          this search path (used by meson-python editable installs); defaults to `None`

      Update the return type annotations of both `_handle_pth_file` and `_handle_editable_module`
      from `list[Path]` to `list[SearchPath]`.

      Each `Path` value that was previously returned directly must now be wrapped in a
      `SearchPath(path=..., always_scan_for=None)`.  The externally visible behavior of the path
      resolution logic itself must not change; only the wrapping layer changes.

      `SearchPath` (or its equivalent) must be exported in `__all__` if it is intended to be part
      of the public API, and must be importable from `griffe.finder`.

requirements.description:
The numpy docstring parser does not recognise "see also", "notes", and "warnings" as named
      sections. These titles are absent from the `_section_kind` mapping.  As a result, content
      under "See Also", "Notes", and "Warnings" headings is never split into its own section object;
      it is silently merged into the preceding text section.  Five tests that expect 2 sections
      (text + admonition) receive only 1 section (combined text) and fail with
      `assert 1 == 2`.
requirements.contract:
Add handling for at least the following section titles in the numpy parser:
        - "see also"  → parsed as a `DocstringSectionAdmonition` with `kind="see-also"` and the
          body lines as `description`; the section `title` must be `"See Also"` (original case).
        - "notes"     → parsed as a `DocstringSectionAdmonition` with `kind="note"` and the body
          as `description`; title `"Notes"`.
        - "warnings"  → parsed as a `DocstringSectionAdmonition` with `kind="warning"` and the
          body as `description`; title `"Warnings"`.

      Behavioral requirements:
        - The body of such an admonition section is everything between the dash line and the next
          recognised section header (or end of docstring), with leading/trailing blank lines
          stripped.  Multi-paragraph bodies (separated by blank lines) must be preserved verbatim
          (e.g. `"some_function\n\nmore text"`).
        - An empty body (nothing between the dash line and EOF/next section) must produce a
          `DocstringSectionAdmonition` whose `value.description == ""`.
        - A dash-line (`---`) that appears in body text (i.e. not immediately below a valid
          section title) must NOT create a new section; it must be treated as literal content.
        - `sections[0].value` must equal the summary text (unchanged).
        - `sections[1].title` must equal the original-case section title (e.g. `"See Also"`).
        - `sections[1].value` must be a `DocstringAdmonition` with the correct `kind` and
          `description` as described above.

requirements.description:
Two related bugs exist in the AST value extraction module:

      1. `_extract_yield` (line 292) returns `repr(None)` (i.e. the string `"None"`) when
         `node.value is None` (bare `yield`), and returns only the value expression string when
         `node.value` is present (e.g. `"a"` for `yield a`). Both cases omit the `"yield"` keyword,
         so `get_value` returns `"None"` instead of `"yield"`, and `"a"` instead of `"yield a"`.

      2. `ast.YieldFrom` is entirely absent from `_node_map`, causing a `KeyError` whenever a
         `yield from` expression is encountered (affects both `get_value` and `safe_get_value`
         paths, and thus also `safe_get_expression` in `expressions.py`).
requirements.contract:
Fix `_extract_yield` so that:
        - `yield` (bare, `node.value is None`) → returns the string `"yield"`
        - `yield a` (`node.value` present) → returns `"yield " + _extract(node.value)`

      Add a new handler `_extract_yield_from` and register it in `_node_map` under `ast.YieldFrom`:
        - `yield from a` → returns `"yield from " + _extract(node.value)`

      The function signatures should mirror the existing extractor pattern:
        `def _extract_yield_from(node: ast.YieldFrom, **kwargs: Any) -> str`

      No changes to the public `get_value` or `safe_get_value` interfaces are needed.

requirements.description:
`temporary_pypackage` accepts only `init: bool` to control whether the leaf package's
      `__init__.py` is created. Tests now call it with an additional keyword argument
      `inits: bool` (e.g. `temporary_pypackage("pkg", [...], init=False, inits=False)`), which
      is intended to control whether `__init__.py` files are created inside subdirectories that
      are generated while creating the listed module files. The missing parameter causes
      `TypeError: temporary_pypackage() got an unexpected keyword argument 'inits'` in 3 tests.
requirements.contract:
Add an `inits: bool = True` keyword-only parameter to `temporary_pypackage`.

      Behavioral contract:
        - When `inits=True` (default, backward-compatible): behaviour is unchanged; subdirectories
          created for module path components receive `__init__.py` files as today.
        - When `inits=False`: subdirectories created while processing entries in the `modules`
          sequence must NOT receive `__init__.py` files (making them namespace packages or plain
          directories).

      The parameter only affects intermediate subdirectories generated for module paths; it does
      not affect whether the root package itself gets an `__init__.py` (that remains controlled by
      the existing `init` parameter).

      The yielded `TmpPackage` object's interface is unchanged.

requirements.description:
When a `Class` object carries the `"dataclass"` label and has no `__init__` member,
      `Class.parameters` builds parameter objects from `self.attributes.values()`. This includes
      attributes whose labels contain `"property"` or `"cached_property"`, which represent
      `@property` and `@cached_property` decorated methods. These are not dataclass fields and
      must not be included as constructor parameters. The test asserts `"a" not in params` and
      `"b" not in params` (where `a` is a `@property` and `b` is a `@cached_property`) but both
      currently appear in the parameter list.
requirements.contract:
In the `"dataclass"` branch of `Class.parameters`, filter out attributes that carry a
      `"property"` or `"cached_property"` label before constructing the `Parameters` object.

      Expected behavior:
        - Only genuine dataclass fields (i.e. attributes without `"property"` or
          `"cached_property"` in their `labels`) are returned as parameters.
        - The relative order of the remaining parameters is preserved.
        - No change to the function signature or return type (`Parameters`).
        - The `__init__`-based path (when `__init__` IS present) is unaffected.

requirements.description:
The _load_extension function must correctly identify and handle absolute POSIX
      file path strings as extension sources. Two sub-cases must work:

      Sub-case A — absolute path without class name
        e.g., "/absolute/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py"
        colons == 0, so extension_name stays None.
        The path is passed directly to os.path.exists and, if it exists, to
        _load_extension_path.  All Extension subclasses found in the module's
        namespace are collected and returned as instances.

      Sub-case B — absolute path with colon-separated class name
        e.g., "/absolute/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest"
        colons == 1 and the colon is NOT part of a Windows drive letter
        (Path(import_path).drive == "" on Linux/Mac), so the condition
            colons > 1 or (colons and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive)
        is True.  The string is split at the last colon:
            import_path  = "/absolute/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py"
            extension_name = "ExtensionTest"
        The file is loaded via _load_extension_path, and the attribute named
        extension_name is retrieved from the resulting module and instantiated.

      The critical invariant is that, after the colon-split logic, import_path must
      refer to an existing file so that os.path.exists(import_path) returns True
      and the file-load branch is taken—not the dynamic_import branch (which
      cannot handle filesystem paths containing slashes).

      Ensure that the colon-split condition and the os.path.exists check are ordered
      and implemented so that both sub-cases above work correctly for arbitrary
      absolute POSIX paths produced by Path(...).absolute().as_posix().
requirements.contract:
Given a string extension specifier that is an absolute POSIX path:

      1. If the specifier contains no colon (or only a Windows drive-letter colon):
         - import_path  := the full specifier string
         - extension_name := None
         - os.path.exists(import_path) must return True for the load branch to execute
         - _load_extension_path(import_path) loads the Python module
         - Every class in the module's namespace that is a strict subclass of
           (VisitorExtension, InspectorExtension, Extension) and is not one of those
           base classes itself is instantiated with **options and returned in a list

      2. If the specifier ends with ":ClassName" where the colon is not a drive letter:
         - The string is split at the LAST colon
         - import_path  := the part before the last colon  (a valid .py file path)
         - extension_name := the part after the last colon  (a class name)
         - os.path.exists(import_path) must return True
         - _load_extension_path(import_path) loads the module
         - getattr(module, extension_name)(**options) returns the single instance

      Error cases:
      - If the file does not exist at import_path after splitting, an
        ExtensionNotLoadedError is raised (via the dynamic_import fallback).
      - If extension_name is given but the attribute does not exist on the loaded
        module, ExtensionNotLoadedError is raised with an informative message.

      The function signature and return type must remain:
        _load_extension(
            extension: str | dict[str, Any] | ExtensionType | type[ExtensionType]
        ) -> ExtensionType | list[ExtensionType]
requirements.acceptance_tests:
The following two parametrized test cases in
      tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions must pass:

        test_loading_extensions[<absolute_path>/tests/test_extensions.py]
        test_loading_extensions[<absolute_path>/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]

      where <absolute_path> is whatever Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute()
      resolves to in the test execution environment (e.g., /app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py
      or /app/code/tests/test_extensions.py).

      For each case the following assertions inside the test must hold:
        1. extensions._extensions[0] exists (the list is non-empty)
        2. extensions._extensions[0].__class__.__name__ == "ExtensionTest"
        3. For the dict-form variants (none of the absolute-path cases are dicts,
           so assertion 3 does not apply here)

      The passing of all OTHER parametrized variants of test_loading_extensions
      (relative paths, module dotted paths, class instances, class objects) must
      not be regressed.

      Additionally, test_extension_events must continue to pass.

requirements.description:
The current colon-splitting logic that separates a file path from an optional
      extension class name does not correctly distinguish between a Windows drive-letter
      colon (e.g., `C:` in `C:/path/to/file.py`) and an extension-name separator colon
      (e.g., the `:ExtensionTest` in `path/to/file.py:ExtensionTest`).

      The naive check `if ":" in import_path` will incorrectly split a bare Windows
      absolute path `C:/path/to/file.py` into `("C", "/path/to/file.py")`, treating
      `/path/to/file.py` as the extension class name. As a result:
      - `os.path.exists("C")` returns False.
      - The code falls through to `dynamic_import("C")`, which raises
        `ExtensionNotLoadedError` or `ModuleNotFoundError`.
      - The test cases that pass an absolute POSIX/Windows path without a class name
        fail, and so do the ones that pass a Windows path with a class name (because
        the split happens at the wrong colon).
requirements.contract:
The colon-splitting logic must satisfy all of the following conditions:

      1. **Absolute POSIX path without class name** (e.g., `/abs/path/file.py`):
         - No colon present → `extension_name` must be `None`.
         - `os.path.exists` is called on the full path; if it exists, the file is
           loaded and all `Extension` subclasses in the module are returned.

      2. **Absolute POSIX path with class name** (e.g., `/abs/path/file.py:ExtensionTest`):
         - One colon present; `Path(import_path).drive` is `""` on POSIX systems.
         - The path must be split on the last colon:
           `import_path = "/abs/path/file.py"`, `extension_name = "ExtensionTest"`.
         - The named class is instantiated (with options if any) and returned.

      3. **Absolute Windows path without class name** (e.g., `C:/abs/path/file.py`):
         - One colon present; `Path(import_path).drive == "C:"`.
         - The drive-letter colon must NOT be treated as a class-name separator.
         - `extension_name` must be `None`.
         - `os.path.exists` is called on the full path and the file is loaded normally.

      4. **Absolute Windows path with class name**
         (e.g., `C:/abs/path/file.py:ExtensionTest`):
         - Two colons present.
         - The path must be split on the LAST colon:
           `import_path = "C:/abs/path/file.py"`, `extension_name = "ExtensionTest"`.
         - The named class is instantiated and returned.

      5. **Relative path with class name** (e.g., `tests/file.py:ExtensionTest`):
         - One colon, `Path(import_path).drive == ""`.
         - Split on the last colon; behavior unchanged from current handling.

      6. **Dotted module path** (e.g., `tests.test_extensions`):
         - No colon → `extension_name = None`; behaviour unchanged.

      The discriminating rule that satisfies all cases above:
      - Count the number of colons (`colons`).
      - Perform the split if and only if:
        `colons > 1` OR (`colons == 1` AND the colon is NOT part of the Windows drive,
        i.e., `":" not in Path(import_path).drive`).
      - In all other cases, `extension_name = None`.

      After splitting, the existing file-load path (`os.path.exists` → `_load_extension_path`)
      and the named-class instantiation path (`getattr(ext_object, extension_name)(**options)`)
      must behave as they do today.

      No changes to the public interface of `load_extensions` or `_load_extension` are
      required.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
The following pytest parametrize cases in
      `tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions` must pass:

      - `test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py]`
        (absolute POSIX path, no class name — corresponds to
        `Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute().as_posix()`)

      - `test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]`
        (absolute POSIX path with class name — corresponds to
        `Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()`)

      Both cases must satisfy the assertion:
        `loaded.__class__.__name__ == "ExtensionTest"`
      and, for the dict variant (`{"path": {"option": 0}}`):
        `loaded.kwargs == {"option": 0}`

      Additionally, all pre-existing parametrize cases (relative paths, dotted module
      paths, class instances, class objects) must continue to pass — i.e., no
      regressions in `tests/test_extensions.py`.

requirements.description:
The `_load_extension` function currently does not reliably support loading
      extensions from absolute file paths, specifically for two sub-cases:

      (a) An absolute POSIX path to a `.py` file without a class name suffix,
          e.g. `/abs/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py`.

      (b) An absolute POSIX path to a `.py` file with a `:`-separated class name
          suffix, e.g. `/abs/path/to/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest`.

      The colon-splitting step that separates a file path from an optional class name
      must correctly distinguish between:
        - A class-name separator colon (appended after the `.py` extension), and
        - A Windows drive-letter colon (e.g. the `C:` in `C:/Users/…`).

      Without this distinction, an absolute Windows-style path processed on any
      platform may be split incorrectly, causing `os.path.exists` to evaluate a
      wrong (truncated) path and the extension module to fail to load.

      After the colon-splitting step, when the resulting `import_path` string
      refers to an existing file on the filesystem, the function must load the
      module from that file (using `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location`) rather
      than attempting a Python-dotted-path import. This behaviour must work for both
      relative and absolute paths.
requirements.contract:
1. **Colon-splitting rule for `filepath:classname` format**

         Given the raw import-path string (after dict-key extraction, if applicable):
         - Count the total number of `:` characters.
         - If the count is greater than 1, OR if the count is exactly 1 AND the
           character `:` does not appear in the drive component of the path
           (i.e. `Path(import_path).drive` does not contain `:`), then split on the
           LAST colon to obtain `(import_path, extension_name)`.
         - Otherwise (single colon that belongs to a Windows drive letter), set
           `extension_name = None` and leave `import_path` unchanged.

      2. **File-path detection and module loading**

         After the colon-splitting step, if `import_path` refers to an existing file
         (`os.path.exists(import_path)` is True), the function must:
         - Load the module from that file using
           `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` with the basename (without
           extension) as the module name.
         - Register the loaded module in `sys.modules` under that name.
         - Raise `ExtensionNotLoadedError` (wrapping any `ImportError`) if the
           module cannot be loaded.

      3. **Class resolution after file loading**

         - If `extension_name` is non-None: retrieve `getattr(module, extension_name)`,
           instantiate it with the provided `options` kwargs, and return the instance.
           Raise `ExtensionNotLoadedError` if the attribute does not exist.
         - If `extension_name` is None: iterate over all names in the module's
           namespace; collect every class that is a subclass of
           `(VisitorExtension, InspectorExtension, Extension)` and is not one of
           those base classes itself; instantiate each with `options`, and return
           the resulting list.

      4. **No-option default**

         When the extension specifier is a plain string (not a dict), `options`
         defaults to `{}`, meaning extensions are instantiated with no arguments
         (unless options are explicitly supplied via the dict form).

      5. **Acceptance of both relative and absolute paths**

         The file-path detection (step 2) must work for:
         - Relative paths (e.g. `tests/test_extensions.py`)
         - Absolute POSIX paths (e.g. `/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py`)
         - Absolute Windows-style paths expressed as POSIX strings
           (e.g. `C:/Users/user/tests/test_extensions.py`)
requirements.acceptance_tests:
Primary acceptance criteria (the two non-passed test cases):

        tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py]
        tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]

      These are the parametrised variants generated by:

        Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute().as_posix()
        Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()

      when the test suite runs from the `/app/codes` working directory.

      For each, the test verifies:
        1. `load_extensions([extension])` returns an `Extensions` object whose
           `_extensions[0].__class__.__name__` equals `"ExtensionTest"`.
        2. When the extension is supplied as a dict (e.g.
           `{"/abs/path/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest": {"option": 0}}`),
           `_extensions[0].kwargs` equals `{"option": 0}`.

      Secondary (regression) acceptance criteria — the following parametrised
      variants must continue to pass:

        test_loading_extensions[tests/test_extensions.py]
        test_loading_extensions[tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]
        test_loading_extensions[tests/test_extensions.py0]   (dict form)
        test_loading_extensions[tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest0]  (dict form)
        test_loading_extensions[tests.test_extensions]
        test_loading_extensions[tests.test_extensions.ExtensionTest]
        test_loading_extensions[ExtensionTest0]  (instance form)
        test_loading_extensions[ExtensionTest1]  (class form)
        test_extension_events

requirements.description:
The _load_extension function must correctly identify and load extension modules from
      absolute file paths (both POSIX-style such as /abs/path/to/file.py and Windows-style
      such as C:/abs/path/to/file.py), including when the path is combined with a class name
      using colon notation (e.g., /abs/path/to/file.py:ClassName).

      Currently, when an absolute POSIX path is supplied (zero colons), the os.path.exists
      check in the elif branch should correctly route to _load_extension_path. However, the
      colon-splitting guard condition:

          colons > 1 or (colons and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive)

      relies on Path.drive returning the Windows drive prefix (e.g., "C:") to avoid splitting
      on the drive letter. On a POSIX system, Path.drive is always "", so this condition
      correctly splits on a single colon for POSIX paths. But for absolute paths passed from
      Windows (e.g., from Path.absolute().as_posix() which emits "C:/..."), evaluated on a
      POSIX host, Path.drive returns "" rather than "C:", causing the condition to split at the
      drive colon, yielding an incorrect import_path = "C" and extension_name = "/...".

      Additionally, loading an extension from an absolute path without a class name must
      reliably discover all Extension subclasses inside the loaded module file even when the
      module's absolute path differs from what the original test collection path was.
requirements.contract:
1. Input recognition:
         - If import_path is an absolute path (starts with "/" on POSIX, or matches
           "[A-Za-z]:/" or "[A-Za-z]:\\" on Windows) AND os.path.exists(import_path) is True,
           it MUST be treated as a filesystem path and loaded via _load_extension_path.
         - A path-with-class-name string of the form "<filepath>:<ClassName>" must be split
           into the file path and class name before the os.path.exists check.

      2. Colon split for filepath:ClassName notation:
         - The splitting logic must correctly distinguish between the drive-letter colon in
           Windows paths (e.g., "C:/path/file.py") and the class-separator colon in
           "file.py:ClassName" notation.
         - Specifically, for a string with exactly one colon:
             * If the substring before the colon is a single ASCII letter (potential Windows
               drive letter, e.g., "C"), do NOT split — treat the colon as part of the drive.
             * In all other cases, split at the colon to obtain (file_path, class_name).
         - For a string with more than one colon:
             * Split at the LAST colon (rsplit(":", 1)) to obtain (file_path, class_name),
               where file_path may itself contain a Windows drive colon.
         - After splitting, the resulting file_path must satisfy os.path.exists(file_path)
           for the extension to be loaded from file.

      3. Loading from absolute path (no class name):
         - When a bare absolute path is given (no colon-based class name), the function must
           load the module via _load_extension_path and then search for all Extension subclasses
           (instances satisfying isclass(obj) and issubclass(obj, ext_classes) and
           obj not in ext_classes) in the module's namespace.
         - The returned list must contain exactly one item per concrete Extension subclass
           defined in that file (not counting imported base classes).

      4. Loading from absolute path with class name:
         - When the input is "<absolute_path>:<ClassName>", load the module from absolute_path
           and return getattr(module, ClassName)(**options).
         - If the attribute does not exist, raise ExtensionNotLoadedError with an informative
           message referencing both the path and the missing class name.

      5. Behavior is platform-independent: the logic must produce correct results whether
         running on Linux, macOS, or Windows.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
Primary test cases (must all pass):
        tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py]
        tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]

      These tests are parametrized with:
        Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute().as_posix()
        Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()

      Each test asserts:
        1. extensions._extensions[0].__class__.__name__ == "ExtensionTest"
        2. For dict-form inputs: loaded.kwargs == {"option": 0}  (not applicable to these two cases)

      Regression: all other parametrized cases of test_loading_extensions must continue to pass:
        - Module path: "tests.test_extensions", {"tests.test_extensions": {"option": 0}}
        - Extension path: "tests.test_extensions.ExtensionTest",
                          {"tests.test_extensions.ExtensionTest": {"option": 0}}
        - Relative filepath: "tests/test_extensions.py",
                              {"tests/test_extensions.py": {"option": 0}}
        - Relative filepath + class: "tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest",
                                      {"tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest": {"option": 0}}
        - Instance: ExtensionTest(option=0)
        - Class: ExtensionTest

      Additionally, tests/test_extensions.py::test_extension_events must remain passing.

requirements.description:
The `_load_extension` function currently uses a simplistic colon-splitting strategy to
      separate a file path from a class name (e.g., "path/to/file.py:ClassName"). This approach
      is broken for Windows-style absolute paths that contain a drive-letter colon (e.g.,
      "C:/path/to/extension.py"), where the single colon after the drive letter must NOT be
      treated as a class-name separator. Additionally, a Windows path combined with a class
      separator ("C:/path/to/extension.py:ClassName") produces two colons, requiring different
      handling than the single-colon case.

      In summary, the two scenarios that need correct handling are:
        1. Absolute POSIX path without class name: "/abs/path/to/ext.py"
           — must be detected as a file via `os.path.exists` and loaded directly.
        2. Absolute path (POSIX or Windows) with class name: "/abs/path/ext.py:ClassName"
           or "C:/abs/path/ext.py:ClassName"
           — must split only on the rightmost (class-separator) colon, preserving the full
             file path including any Windows drive-letter prefix.
requirements.contract:
The `_load_extension` function, after resolving `import_path` from the raw extension
      specification, must apply the following colon-disambiguation logic before checking
      whether `import_path` refers to a file on disk:

      1. Count the number of colons in `import_path`.

      2. If there are zero colons: set `extension_name = None`; `import_path` is unchanged.

      3. If there is exactly one colon:
         - Split `import_path` on the colon into `(before, after)`.
         - If `before` is a single ASCII alphabetic character (Windows drive letter, e.g. "C"),
           treat the whole string as a bare file path: set `extension_name = None` and leave
           `import_path` unchanged.
         - Otherwise, treat it as "filepath:ClassName": set `import_path = before` and
           `extension_name = after`.

      4. If there are two or more colons (Windows path + class separator):
         - Split from the right on the last colon: `import_path, extension_name = rsplit(":", 1)`.
         - This preserves the drive-letter prefix (e.g. "C:") as part of `import_path`.

      After the colon disambiguation, the function must check `os.path.exists(import_path)`.
      If the file exists, load it as a Python module using `spec_from_file_location` /
      `module_from_spec`. If `extension_name` is provided, retrieve that attribute from the
      module and instantiate it with the resolved options. If `extension_name` is absent,
      scan the module's namespace for all concrete subclasses of `Extension`,
      `VisitorExtension`, or `InspectorExtension`, instantiate each with the resolved options,
      and return the list.

      Interface summary:
        Input:  `import_path: str` — may be a dotted module name, a relative/absolute file
                path, or a "filepath:ClassName" string (POSIX or Windows).
        Output: one `ExtensionType` instance, or a `list[ExtensionType]` when no class name
                is given and multiple Extension subclasses are found in the module.
        Raises: `ExtensionNotLoadedError` — when the file cannot be loaded or the named
                class attribute does not exist on the loaded module.

      Boundary conditions:
        - A single-letter alphabetic character before a lone colon must always be interpreted
          as a Windows drive letter, never as a class separator.
        - Absolute POSIX paths (starting with "/") have zero colons in the path segment
          itself; they are handled by the zero-colon branch without any splitting.
requirements.acceptance_tests:
The following test node IDs (from code/tests/test_extensions.py) must pass:

        tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py]
        tests/test_extensions.py::test_loading_extensions[/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest]

      These correspond to the parametrize values:
        - `Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute().as_posix()`
          → an absolute POSIX path with no colon; `load_extensions` must load the module from
            disk and find the `ExtensionTest` class automatically.
        - `Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()`
          → an absolute path string with a trailing ":ExtensionTest"; `load_extensions` must
            correctly parse the file path and class name, load the module from disk, and
            instantiate `ExtensionTest`.

      In both cases the assertion is:
        loaded.__class__.__name__ == "ExtensionTest"

      For the dict-variant parametrize cases (where options are provided), the additional
      assertion `loaded.kwargs == {"option": 0}` must also hold.

      Custom verification: manually invoke `load_extensions` with the absolute path of
      `tests/test_extensions.py` and confirm that `._extensions[0].__class__.__name__`
      equals `"ExtensionTest"`. Repeat with the ":ExtensionTest" suffix appended.

requirements.description:
The `_load_extension` function does not correctly handle absolute file paths
      (POSIX or Windows) that use a colon as a class-name separator.

      When an extension is specified as an absolute path with an optional class name
      (e.g., `/abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest`), the function must:
      1. Distinguish the class-name separator colon from a Windows drive-letter colon
         (e.g., `C:/path/to/file.py`).
      2. After splitting, verify that the file portion of the path exists on disk
         via `os.path.exists`, and load it via `_load_extension_path`.
      3. When no class name is given (e.g., `/abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py`),
         discover and instantiate all `Extension`/`VisitorExtension`/`InspectorExtension`
         subclasses found in the module.

      Currently, the colon-parsing branch does not reliably differentiate these cases,
      which causes the `os.path.exists` check to be performed on the wrong string
      (either the un-split path that contains the class suffix, or the drive-letter prefix
      on Windows), leading to failure when loading extensions from absolute file paths.
requirements.contract:
The updated `_load_extension` function must implement the following colon-handling
      logic before the `os.path.exists` check:

      1. Count the total number of colons in `import_path`.
      2. If there is exactly **one** colon:
         - Split into `before` and `after` at the colon.
         - If `before` is a single ASCII alphabetic character (Windows drive letter,
           e.g., `C`), treat the entire original `import_path` as a plain file path
           and set `extension_name = None`.
         - Otherwise, treat `before` as the file path and `after` as the class name:
           set `import_path = before` and `extension_name = after`.
      3. If there are **more than one** colon (Windows path with class name, e.g.,
         `C:/path/to/file.py:ClassName`):
         - Split at the **last** colon (using `rsplit(":", 1)`).
         - The left part is the file path; the right part is the class name.
      4. If there are **no** colons, set `extension_name = None` and leave
         `import_path` unchanged.

      After this preprocessing, the subsequent `os.path.exists(import_path)` check
      operates on the clean file path (without any class-name suffix), allowing correct
      file-based loading on both POSIX and Windows systems.

      Function signature remains unchanged:
        `_load_extension(extension: str | dict[str, Any] | ExtensionType | type[ExtensionType])
         -> ExtensionType | list[ExtensionType]`

      Observable behavioral contracts:
      - `_load_extension("/abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py")` must load all
        Extension subclasses found in that file and return them as a list.
      - `_load_extension("/abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest")` must
        load the file, retrieve the `ExtensionTest` attribute, instantiate it (with no
        options), and return the instance.
      - `_load_extension({"abs/path/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest": {"option": 0}})`
        must instantiate `ExtensionTest` with `kwargs = {"option": 0}`.
      - `_load_extension("C:/path/to/ext.py")` (Windows drive letter) must NOT treat
        `"C"` as the file path; the whole `C:/path/to/ext.py` string must be passed
        to `os.path.exists`.
      - `_load_extension("C:/path/to/ext.py:MyExt")` (Windows drive + class) must
        correctly split into file path `C:/path/to/ext.py` and class name `MyExt`.

requirements.description:
The `_load_extension` function must correctly handle absolute file paths
      (both POSIX-style, e.g. `/abs/path/ext.py`, and Windows-style, e.g.
      `C:/abs/path/ext.py`) passed as extension specifications, with or without
      the `filepath:ClassName` class-name notation.

      The core problem is colon disambiguation: a Windows absolute path such as
      `C:/path/ext.py` contains a colon that is a drive-letter separator, NOT a
      class-name separator. Naively splitting on `:` would yield
      `import_path = "C"` and `extension_name = "/path/ext.py"`, which is
      incorrect. At the same time, the notation `filepath:ClassName` (e.g.
      `tests/ext.py:MyExt` or `/abs/path/ext.py:MyExt`) uses a colon as a
      legitimate class-name separator.

      The colon-splitting step must therefore:
      1. Identify a lone colon preceded by exactly one ASCII alpha character as a
         Windows drive-letter colon (leave `import_path` unchanged, set
         `extension_name = None`).
      2. Identify any other single colon as a `filepath:ClassName` separator
         (split `import_path` into path and class name).
      3. When more than one colon is present (e.g. Windows path WITH class name:
         `C:/path/ext.py:ClassName`, colons == 2), use `rsplit(":", 1)` to
         extract only the last colon-separated class name.

      Additionally, the `os.path.exists(import_path)` file-existence check must
      be performed AFTER the colon-stripping step, so that it operates on the
      clean file path (without the appended `:ClassName`), and must work for
      both relative and absolute paths.
requirements.contract:
Function signature (unchanged): `_load_extension(extension) -> ExtensionType | list[ExtensionType]`

      Input scenarios that must be handled correctly:

      a) Absolute POSIX path, no class name:
         input:  "/abs/path/tests/ext.py"
         result: all Extension subclasses found in the module are instantiated
                 and returned as a list.

      b) Absolute POSIX path with class name:
         input:  "/abs/path/tests/ext.py:MyExtension"
         result: `MyExtension` is retrieved from the loaded module, instantiated
                 with `options`, and returned as a single instance.

      c) Absolute Windows path, no class name (drive-letter colon only):
         input:  "C:/abs/path/tests/ext.py"
         result: treated as a plain file path; all Extension subclasses in the
                 module are instantiated and returned.
         constraint: the colon in "C:" must NOT be interpreted as a class-name
                     separator; `import_path` must remain "C:/abs/path/tests/ext.py".

      d) Absolute Windows path with class name (two colons):
         input:  "C:/abs/path/tests/ext.py:MyExtension"
         result: `MyExtension` instantiated from the loaded module.
         constraint: only the LAST colon separates the class name.

      In all cases, the loaded module must be identified via `os.path.exists(import_path)`
      (checked after colon stripping), and loaded via `_load_extension_path`.
      The `dynamic_import` fallback must NOT be invoked for paths that point to
      an existing file.

requirements.description:
The current implementation of `_load_extension` in `base.py` is missing
      the `from pathlib import Path` import and uses a custom single-character
      ASCII-alpha heuristic to distinguish Windows drive-letter colons from
      class-separator colons in extension import paths.  The custom logic
      replicates the intent of using `Path.drive` but is not the canonical
      implementation: it splits on the first colon (`split(":", 1)`) and
      checks whether the left-hand side is exactly one ASCII alphabetical
      character.  The target implementation instead uses
      `Path(import_path).drive` — which is `""` on POSIX systems and `"C:"`
      (or similar) on Windows — to decide whether the colon is part of a drive
      letter.  Additionally, the current logic uses a multi-branch
      `if / elif / else` structure instead of the more concise single
      conditional expression used in the target.
requirements.contract:
The `_load_extension` function must correctly resolve the `extension_name`
      (class-name suffix) and the actual `import_path` (file path or dotted
      module path) for all of the following input forms:

      1. POSIX absolute file path without class specifier, e.g.
         `/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py` — zero colons → `extension_name`
         must be `None`, `import_path` must remain unchanged.

      2. POSIX absolute file path with class specifier, e.g.
         `/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest` — one colon, the
         drive component is empty on POSIX → `import_path` must be split at the
         last colon, yielding
         `import_path = "/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py"` and
         `extension_name = "ExtensionTest"`.

      3. Windows absolute path without class specifier, e.g.
         `C:/path/to/extension.py` — one colon that is part of the drive letter
         (`Path(import_path).drive == "C:"`, which contains `":"`), so the
         colon must NOT be treated as a class separator → `extension_name` must
         be `None`, `import_path` must remain unchanged.

      4. Windows absolute path with class specifier, e.g.
         `C:/path/to/extension.py:MyExtension` — two colons →
         `import_path` must be obtained via `rsplit(":", 1)`, yielding
         `import_path = "C:/path/to/extension.py"` and
         `extension_name = "MyExtension"`.

      5. Dotted Python import path with no colon, e.g.
         `my.package.ExtensionClass` → `extension_name` must be `None`.

      The detection logic MUST use `pathlib.Path(import_path).drive` to
      determine whether a lone colon is a Windows drive-letter separator.
      Concretely, the condition for splitting should be:

          colons > 1  OR  (colons == 1  AND  ":" not in Path(import_path).drive)

      When this condition is True, the split must be performed with
      `import_path.rsplit(":", 1)`, assigning the two parts to `import_path`
      and `extension_name` respectively.  When it is False, `extension_name`
      must be set to `None`.

      `pathlib.Path` must be imported at the top of the module (not inside
      the function) to support this logic.

      No other behaviour of `_load_extension` or any other function in the
      module may change.

requirements.description:
The current colon-detection condition used to distinguish a Windows drive-letter colon
      (e.g., "C:/path/to/file.py") from a class-separator colon (e.g., "file.py:ClassName")
      reads:

        colons = import_path.count(":")
        if colons > 1 or (colons == 1 and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive):

      This block is accompanied by four comment lines that describe an approach ("check directly:
      a single ASCII letter before a lone colon means a Windows drive letter") that is NOT
      actually implemented by the code — the code uses Path(import_path).drive, which the
      comments themselves describe as "unreliable cross-platform". The comments are therefore
      misleading and inaccurate.

      Additionally, the sub-condition `colons == 1` is unnecessarily restrictive compared to the
      simpler and more idiomatic truthiness check `colons`. Because the outer `colons > 1` branch
      is evaluated first (short-circuit OR), the only cases that reach the second operand are
      colons == 0 and colons == 1; for those cases `colons == 1` and `colons` behave identically,
      but the `== 1` form obscures intent and is inconsistent with the original (and correct)
      implementation.

      The parameterized test cases that load extensions from absolute file paths
      (Path("tests/test_extensions.py").absolute().as_posix() and
      Path("tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest").absolute().as_posix()) exercise exactly
      this code path. When the colon-detection logic does not cleanly split an absolute POSIX
      path containing a class-separator colon, the extension fails to load with the correct
      class name, causing both absolute-path parametrize variants to fail.
requirements.contract:
The function _load_extension(extension) in src/griffe/extensions/base.py must correctly
      parse an import_path string that encodes both a file path and an optional class name
      separated by a colon (":"), under all of the following scenarios:

      1. Absolute POSIX path without class name, e.g. "/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py"
         — colons == 0; extension_name must be set to None; the path is used as-is for
           os.path.exists() and subsequent module loading.

      2. Absolute POSIX path with class name, e.g. "/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest"
         — colons == 1; Path(...).drive is "" on POSIX systems, so ":" not in drive is True;
           the string must be split at the last ":" to yield
           import_path = "/app/codes/tests/test_extensions.py" and
           extension_name = "ExtensionTest".

      3. Relative path without class name, e.g. "tests/test_extensions.py"
         — colons == 0; extension_name must be None.

      4. Relative path with class name, e.g. "tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest"
         — colons == 1; drive is ""; must split correctly.

      5. Windows absolute path without class name, e.g. "C:/tests/test_extensions.py"
         — colons == 1; Path(...).drive == "C:" on Windows; ":" in drive is True, so
           the condition must evaluate to False; extension_name must remain None.

      6. Windows absolute path with class name, e.g. "C:/tests/test_extensions.py:ExtensionTest"
         — colons == 2; colons > 1 is True (short-circuit); must split at the last ":"
           to yield import_path = "C:/tests/test_extensions.py" and
           extension_name = "ExtensionTest".

      The required change is:
        • Remove the four inaccurate comment lines that describe an approach different from
          the actual implementation.
        • Replace the sub-condition `colons == 1` with the truthiness check `colons` so the
          condition becomes:
              if colons > 1 or (colons and ":" not in Path(import_path).drive):
          This is consistent with the original correct implementation and makes the intent
          clearer: "if there is at least one colon AND the path's drive component does not
          contain a colon, then split".

      No other logic in _load_extension should be altered.
